content.text: Why PoC Exit Criteria Matter

The gap between a proof of concept and a production GenAI feature is wider than in conventional software. A PoC demonstrates that an AI capability is technically achievable; it does not demonstrate that it is safe, cost-controlled, auditable, or compliant. Many enterprise GenAI projects have shipped PoCs to production without a formal exit gate — with the result that PII logging policies were wrong, hallucination rates were only measured on synthetic data, and rollback procedures did not exist when the first incident occurred.

Exit criteria formalise the difference between 'this works in a demo' and 'this is safe to run against real customers.' They are agreed before the PoC begins, not after — agreeing them retroactively creates pressure to lower the bar to fit what was already built.

attrs.alt: Six PoC exit criteria domains with example checklist items

content.text: The Six Exit Criteria Domains
Domain
What it gates
Who owns sign-off
Technical viability
Accuracy, latency, integration, fallback — does the system perform at production scale?
Engineering + AI Quality
Data & privacy
PII handling, retention, residency, provider data use — is data handled lawfully?
DPO + Information Security
Safety & guardrails
Red-team results, PII leak rate, scope violation rate, adversarial robustness
Information Security + AI Quality
Operational readiness
Monitoring, alerting, runbooks, cost controls — can the team operate this in production?
SRE + FinOps
Governance & compliance
Legal sign-off, DPIA, fairness audit, explainability documentation
Legal + DPO + AI Ethics
Business value
Pilot outcome metric results — has the feature demonstrated measurable value?
Product + Sponsor

A passing result in five of six domains does not mean the feature can go to production. Every blocking criterion in every domain must pass. The domains are not weighted — a PII handling failure blocks go-live regardless of excellent technical performance.
Checklist Dataclasses

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional
from enum import Enum

class CheckStatus(str, Enum):
    PASS    = "pass"
    FAIL    = "fail"
    PARTIAL = "partial"   # met with caveats; requires sponsor waiver
    PENDING = "pending"

class Domain(str, Enum):
    TECHNICAL   = "technical_viability"
    DATA        = "data_privacy"
    SAFETY      = "safety_guardrails"
    OPERATIONS  = "operational_readiness"
    GOVERNANCE  = "governance_compliance"
    BUSINESS    = "business_value"

@dataclass
class CheckItem:
    check_id: str
    domain: Domain
    description: str
    is_blocking: bool         # if True, FAIL or PENDING blocks go-live
    evidence_required: str    # what artefact proves this is met
    owner: str
    status: CheckStatus = CheckStatus.PENDING
    evidence_link: Optional[str] = None
    waiver_reason: Optional[str] = None   # only allowed if is_blocking=False

@dataclass
class PoCExitChecklist:
    feature_name: str
    poc_version: str
    items: list[CheckItem] = field(default_factory=list)

    def by_domain(self, domain: Domain) -> list[CheckItem]:
        return [i for i in self.items if i.domain == domain]

    def blocking_failures(self) -> list[CheckItem]:
        return [
            i for i in self.items
            if i.is_blocking and i.status in (CheckStatus.FAIL, CheckStatus.PENDING)
        ]

    def can_proceed(self) -> bool:
        return len(self.blocking_failures()) == 0

    def readiness_score(self) -> float:
        passed = sum(1 for i in self.items if i.status == CheckStatus.PASS)
        return passed / len(self.items) if self.items else 0.0

    def summary(self) -> str:
        total = len(self.items)
        passed = sum(1 for i in self.items if i.status == CheckStatus.PASS)
        blocking = len(self.blocking_failures())
        return (
            f"{self.feature_name} v{self.poc_version}: "
            f"{passed}/{total} checks passed, "
            f"{blocking} blocking failures, "
            f"proceed={self.can_proceed()}"
        )

fnol_checklist = PoCExitChecklist(
    feature_name="FNOL Claims Chatbot",
    poc_version="PoC-1.2",
    items=[

        # ── Technical Viability ───────────────────────────────────────────────
        CheckItem(
            check_id="TECH-01",
            domain=Domain.TECHNICAL,
            description="Hallucination rate ≤ 2% on production-sampled eval set (n=100)",
            is_blocking=True,
            evidence_required="LLM-judge eval report signed by AI Quality Lead",
            owner="AI Quality Team",
            status=CheckStatus.PASS,
            evidence_link="eval-reports/fnol-hallucination-poc12.pdf",
        ),
        CheckItem(
            check_id="TECH-02",
            domain=Domain.TECHNICAL,
            description="TTFT P95 ≤ 800 ms at 3× expected peak load",
            is_blocking=True,
            evidence_required="Load test report from staging at 3× peak sessions",
            owner="Platform Engineering",
            status=CheckStatus.PASS,
            evidence_link="perf-reports/fnol-load-test-poc12.html",
        ),
        CheckItem(
            check_id="TECH-03",
            domain=Domain.TECHNICAL,
            description="CRM integration tested end-to-end on staging with realistic claim volumes",
            is_blocking=True,
            evidence_required="Integration test run log; UAT sign-off from Claims Ops",
            owner="Engineering",
            status=CheckStatus.PARTIAL,
            waiver_reason=None,   # PARTIAL blocks; needs Claims Ops UAT before exit
        ),

        # ── Data & Privacy ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        CheckItem(
            check_id="DATA-01",
            domain=Domain.DATA,
            description="DPA addendum with LLM provider includes zero-retention clause",
            is_blocking=True,
            evidence_required="Signed DPA addendum on file with Legal",
            owner="Legal / DPO",
            status=CheckStatus.PASS,
            evidence_link="legal/provider-dpa-addendum-signed.pdf",
        ),
        CheckItem(
            check_id="DATA-02",
            domain=Domain.DATA,
            description="Prompt log retention policy ≤ 30 days configured and verified in prod environment",
            is_blocking=True,
            evidence_required="Infrastructure config review + retention policy enforcement test",
            owner="Information Security",
            status=CheckStatus.PENDING,
        ),

        # ── Safety & Guardrails ───────────────────────────────────────────────
        CheckItem(
            check_id="SAFE-01",
            domain=Domain.SAFETY,
            description="Red-team suite (200 adversarial prompts) passed with 0 critical safety failures",
            is_blocking=True,
            evidence_required="Red-team report with all test cases and outcomes",
            owner="Information Security + AI Quality",
            status=CheckStatus.PASS,
            evidence_link="security/red-team-poc12.pdf",
        ),
        CheckItem(
            check_id="SAFE-02",
            domain=Domain.SAFETY,
            description="PII leak rate = 0 on 10,000 synthetic interaction DLP scan",
            is_blocking=True,
            evidence_required="DLP scan report from staging",
            owner="Information Security",
            status=CheckStatus.PASS,
            evidence_link="security/dlp-scan-poc12.pdf",
        ),

        # ── Operational Readiness ─────────────────────────────────────────────
        CheckItem(
            check_id="OPS-01",
            domain=Domain.OPERATIONS,
            description="On-call runbook written, reviewed, and accessible in incident management system",
            is_blocking=True,
            evidence_required="Runbook document in Confluence; SRE review sign-off",
            owner="SRE",
            status=CheckStatus.PASS,
        ),
        CheckItem(
            check_id="OPS-02",
            domain=Domain.OPERATIONS,
            description="Monthly cost ceiling enforced; billing alert at 80% of ceiling active",
            is_blocking=False,   # advisory — cost overrun is recoverable
            evidence_required="FinOps dashboard screenshot; alert configuration",
            owner="FinOps",
            status=CheckStatus.PENDING,
        ),

        # ── Governance ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        CheckItem(
            check_id="GOV-01",
            domain=Domain.GOVERNANCE,
            description="DPIA complete and signed by DPO",
            is_blocking=True,
            evidence_required="Signed DPIA on file",
            owner="DPO",
            status=CheckStatus.PASS,
            evidence_link="legal/dpia-fnol-signed.pdf",
        ),

        # ── Business Value ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        CheckItem(
            check_id="BIZ-01",
            domain=Domain.BUSINESS,
            description="Pilot (20 users, 4 weeks) showed rework rate reduction ≥ 30% vs control group",
            is_blocking=True,
            evidence_required="Pilot analysis report with statistical significance (p < 0.05)",
            owner="Product + Data Analytics",
            status=CheckStatus.PASS,
            evidence_link="analytics/fnol-pilot-results-poc12.pdf",
        ),
    ],
)

print(fnol_checklist.summary())
# FNOL Claims Chatbot vPoC-1.2: 8/11 checks passed, 2 blocking failures, proceed=False

blocking = fnol_checklist.blocking_failures()
for b in blocking:
    print(f"{b.check_id}: {b.description[:60]}... [{b.status}]")
# TECH-03: CRM integration tested end-to-end on staging with re... [partial]
# DATA-02: Prompt log retention policy ≤ 30 days configured and... [pending]
PoC Practices That Are Not Production-Ready

Most GenAI PoCs are built under time pressure with technical debt that is accepted during the PoC phase but must be cleared before production. The most common production-readiness gaps are structural: they cannot be fixed by tuning the model or adjusting a prompt.
PoC anti-pattern
Production blocker it creates
Typical remediation effort
Hardcoded API key in code
Security incident if code is ever exposed
1–2 days (secret manager integration)
Single LLM provider, no fallback
Feature unavailable during provider outages
3–5 days (secondary provider + circuit breaker)
No token budget enforcement
Cost spike during traffic surge; no visibility
1–2 days (budget middleware + billing alerts)
Accuracy measured on test set only
Production performance unknown until post-launch incidents
1–2 weeks (production eval pipeline setup)
No pseudonymised audit log
Cannot investigate complaints or regulatory requests
1–3 days (logging middleware + retention policy)
Manual deployment
Inconsistent deployments; no rollback capability
1–2 weeks (CI/CD pipeline + rollback runbook)
Setting Exit Criteria Before the PoC Begins

Exit criteria must be agreed by all domain owners before development of the PoC starts. Setting them after the PoC is built creates a negotiation dynamic: if a criterion is not met, there is pressure to lower it to fit the PoC's actual performance rather than fix the PoC.
Convene a pre-PoC criteria workshop: Product, Engineering, Legal, DPO, InfoSec, and the business sponsor.
For each domain, agree the minimum acceptable standard for production — not for the PoC itself.
Classify each criterion as blocking (must pass for go-live) or advisory (must be tracked but does not block).
Assign an owner to each criterion: the individual responsible for producing the evidence that the criterion is met.
Agree the evidence artefact for each criterion: load test report, signed DPIA, red-team report, LLM-judge eval output.
Record the agreed criteria in a shared document and have all domain owners sign off before PoC development begins.

We built a great PoC in 6 weeks. Then we spent 4 months trying to get Legal and InfoSec to sign off on things nobody had asked them about during the build. If we'd agreed the exit criteria upfront, we'd have known to involve them from the start.
Go / No-Go Decision Record

The go/no-go decision is a formal business decision, not an engineering judgment. It requires sponsor sign-off and a documented record of which criteria passed, which failed, and what conditions must be met before go-live if the decision is 'Conditional Go'.

'Conditional Go' is the most common outcome: most PoC exit reviews find 1–2 criteria that are close but not complete. A Conditional Go is legitimate if the conditions are specific, owned, and time-bounded. It is not legitimate if the conditions are vague ('ensure legal is comfortable') or open-ended ('when engineering has capacity').
Rollback Plan and Trigger Criteria

Every production GenAI feature must have a documented rollback plan agreed before launch. The rollback plan specifies: what constitutes a failure requiring rollback, who makes the rollback decision, how rollback is executed, and what the fallback mode is while the rollback is in effect.

# Rollback trigger evaluation: automated monitoring for production safety net

ROLLBACK_TRIGGERS = [
    {
        "metric": "hallucination_rate",
        "threshold": 0.05,        # if > 5% in a single day
        "direction": "lower_better",
        "consequence": "CRITICAL — immediate rollback; notify AI Quality Lead and DPO",
        "measurement": "LLM-judge on daily 50-case sample; result by 09:00 next day",
    },
    {
        "metric": "rework_rate",
        "threshold": 0.25,        # if > 25% in a single day (baseline was 22%)
        "direction": "lower_better",
        "consequence": "CRITICAL — immediate rollback if sustained 2 days; alert Claims Ops Director",
        "measurement": "CRM rework flag count / AI-handled claims; daily report",
    },
    {
        "metric": "pii_in_output_incidents",
        "threshold": 1,           # if ANY PII incident
        "direction": "lower_better",
        "consequence": "CRITICAL — immediate suspension; notify DPO + IS; mandatory post-incident review",
        "measurement": "DLP scanner continuous; daily audit confirmation",
    },
    {
        "metric": "escalation_rate",
        "threshold": 0.40,        # if > 40% escalating to human (well above 15% target)
        "direction": "lower_better",
        "consequence": "WARNING — investigate root cause; no immediate rollback but escalate to Product",
        "measurement": "Session logs: escalation events / total sessions; hourly",
    },
]

def evaluate_rollback(daily_metrics: dict) -> list[str]:
    """
    Returns list of triggered rollback criteria.
    Empty list means all clear.
    """
    triggered = []
    for trigger in ROLLBACK_TRIGGERS:
        metric = trigger["metric"]
        value = daily_metrics.get(metric)
        if value is None:
            triggered.append(f"MISSING METRIC: {metric} — treat as WARNING")
            continue
        if trigger["direction"] == "lower_better" and value > trigger["threshold"]:
            triggered.append(
                f"TRIGGERED: {metric}={value:.4f} > threshold={trigger['threshold']} "
                f"— {trigger['consequence']}"
            )
    return triggered

# Example usage:
daily = {
    "hallucination_rate": 0.031,
    "rework_rate": 0.11,
    "pii_in_output_incidents": 0,
    "escalation_rate": 0.14,
}
alerts = evaluate_rollback(daily)
# [] — all clear; hallucination at 3.1% is below 5% rollback threshold
# (but above 2% NFR — this would trigger an advisory alert separately)

Rollback triggers must be agreed by the operations team and the business sponsor before go-live. The most common failure is agreeing them after the first incident, when pressure exists to avoid admitting the feature needs to be rolled back. Document triggers quantitatively — not 'if quality deteriorates' but 'if hallucination rate exceeds 5% in a single day's measurement.'
Pilot Design as an Exit Criterion

The business value domain requires pilot results, not projections. A pilot is a controlled deployment to a representative user group (typically 10–30% of the target user population) for a defined period (typically 4–8 weeks). The pilot measures outcome metrics against a control group or a before/after baseline.
Pilot design element
Requirement
FNOL example
User group
Representative cross-section of target users; includes sceptics
15 claims handlers from 3 teams; mix of senior and junior
Control group
Comparable users not using the AI feature, or pre-AI baseline
Historical matched claims processed without AI (8-week lookback)
Outcome metrics measured
At least the north-star KPI driver metrics
Rework rate, time to first response, claimant satisfaction
Duration
Long enough to capture natural variation; ≥ 4 weeks
6 weeks (includes seasonal variation in claim types)
Statistical significance
p < 0.05 for primary outcome metric
Rework rate difference tested with two-proportion z-test
Exit threshold
Pre-agreed minimum effect size for go decision
Rework rate reduction ≥ 30% vs control; p < 0.05

A pilot that meets the statistical significance threshold but shows only marginal improvement (e.g. 3% rework rate reduction vs 30% target) is a business failure signal, not a success. The exit criterion must specify minimum effect size, not just statistical significance.
Common PoC Exit Failures
Failure
Consequence
Prevention
Exit criteria agreed after PoC is built
Criteria lowered to fit PoC; production issues surface later
Workshop before PoC development; signed agreement
Only technical criteria gated
Legal, privacy, and compliance gaps emerge post-launch
All six domains required; legal and DPO in criteria workshop
Blocking / advisory classification inconsistent
Safety criteria treated as advisory; incidents reach production
Explicit blocking/advisory field per criterion; InfoSec and DPO own safety gate classification
No rollback plan before go-live
First incident reveals no agreed rollback procedure; downtime extends
Rollback plan required as OPS domain exit criterion
Pilot measures output metrics only
Business case unvalidated; cost reduction not demonstrated
Pilot must measure at least one outcome metric vs control
Conditional Go conditions left open
'Sort it out before launch' conditions never resolved
Each condition must have a named owner, specific artefact, and date
Summary

PoC exit criteria formalise the difference between a demonstration and a production system across six domains: technical viability, data and privacy, safety and guardrails, operational readiness, governance and compliance, and business value. All blocking criteria across all domains must pass before go-live — domains are not weighted, and a privacy failure blocks regardless of technical excellence. Criteria must be agreed before the PoC is built to prevent the negotiation dynamic that lowers bars to fit existing work. The `PoCExitChecklist.can_proceed()` method embodies the gate: if any blocking item is FAIL or PENDING, the feature does not go to production. A Conditional Go is legitimate only when conditions are specific, owned, and time-bounded. A rollback plan with quantitative trigger criteria is itself an exit criterion, not an optional post-launch task.
